subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: How g changes with height, depth and latitude
summary: Above the surface g falls as an inverse square with distance from the centre; below the surface it falls linearly to zero at the centre; rotation makes g largest at the poles.
body:
**Must know**
- Height $h$: $g_h=g\left(\dfrac{R}{R+h}\right)^2$; for $h\ll R$, $g_h\approx g\left(1-\dfrac{2h}{R}\right)$.
- Depth $d$: $g_d=g\left(1-\dfrac{d}{R}\right)$ (uniform density), reaching zero at the centre.
- Rotation/latitude $\lambda$: $g_\lambda=g-\omega^2R\cos^2\lambda$.
**Exam traps**
- Above: inverse-square decrease. Below: linear decrease. Both reduce g.
- $g$ is maximum at the poles ($\lambda=90^\circ$) and minimum at the equator.

subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: How Earth's mass and radius set g at the surface
summary: The acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface is g = GM/Rôý, which ties g to Earth's mass and radius and is independent of the test mass.
body:
**Must know**
- $g=\dfrac{GM}{R^2}\approx9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$.
- In terms of mean density: $g=\dfrac{4}{3}\pi G R\rho$.
- $g$ is the same for all bodies (independent of the falling mass).
**Exam traps**
- Weight $W=mg$ changes with location because $g$ does, even though mass does not.
- $g$ grows with planet mass and falls with the square of its radius.

formulas.meaning: Acceleration due to gravity at the surface
g in terms of mean density

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: How a satellite's orbital speed and period depend on radius
summary: A satellite in a circular orbit of radius r has orbital speed vã = ã(GM/r) and period T = 2üã(rô°/GM), so Tôý ã rô°.
body:
**Must know**
- Orbital speed: $v_o=\sqrt{\dfrac{GM}{r}}$ (about $7.9\ \text{km s}^{-1}$ near the surface).
- Period: $T=2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{r^3}{GM}}$, hence $T^2\propto r^3$.
- A geostationary satellite has $T=24$ h, lies above the equator at $\approx36000$ km altitude.
**Exam traps**
- A lower orbit means a higher speed; orbital speed is independent of the satellite's mass.

formulas.meaning: Kepler's third law for circular orbits

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: Why an orbiting satellite's total energy is ãGMm/2r
summary: In a circular orbit KE = +GMm/2r and PE = ãGMm/r, so the total energy is E = ãGMm/2r ã negative, marking a bound orbit; its magnitude is the binding energy.
body:
**Must know**
- $KE=\dfrac{GMm}{2r}$, $PE=-\dfrac{GMm}{r}$, so $E=KE+PE=-\dfrac{GMm}{2r}$.
- Total energy is negative (bound orbit); $|E|$ is the binding energy.
- Note $E=-KE$ and $PE=2E$.
**Exam traps**
- The total mechanical energy of a bound orbit is negative.
- To move a satellite to a higher orbit you must supply energy (E becomes less negative).

formulas.meaning: Total energy in a circular orbit

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: How fast a body must move to escape a planet
summary: Escape speed is the minimum speed needed to leave a body's gravity with no further propulsion: vã = ã(2GM/R) = ã(2gR), about 11.2 km/s for Earth.
body:
**Must know**
- $v_e=\sqrt{\dfrac{2GM}{R}}=\sqrt{2gR}\approx11.2\ \text{km s}^{-1}$ for Earth.
- It is independent of the escaping body's mass and of the launch direction.
- It is $\sqrt{2}$ times the orbital speed at the surface: $v_e=\sqrt{2}\,v_o$.
**Exam traps**
- Escape speed does not depend on the projectile's mass.
- Compare carefully: $v_e=\sqrt{2}\,v_{orbital}$.

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: Why gravitational potential energy is ãGMm/r
summary: With the zero taken at infinity, the gravitational PE of two masses is U = ãGMm/r (negative, because gravity is attractive); the gravitational potential is V = ãGM/r.
body:
**Must know**
- Potential energy: $U=-\dfrac{GMm}{r}$ (reference at infinity).
- Gravitational potential: $V=-\dfrac{GM}{r}$, with $U=mV$.
- Near the surface (small $h$): $\Delta U\approx mgh$.
**Exam traps**
- $U$ is negative and increases toward zero as $r$ grows.
- $mgh$ is valid only near the surface where $g$ is essentially constant.

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: How Kepler's three laws describe planetary orbits
summary: Kepler: (1) orbits are ellipses with the Sun at a focus; (2) the radius vector sweeps equal areas in equal times (angular-momentum conservation); (3) Tôý ã aô°.
body:
**Must know**
- Law of orbits: each planet moves in an ellipse with the Sun at one focus.
- Law of areas: $\dfrac{dA}{dt}=\dfrac{L}{2m}=\text{constant}$, so a planet moves fastest at perihelion.
- Law of periods: $T^2\propto a^3$, where $a$ is the semi-major axis.
**Exam traps**
- The equal-area law is a direct consequence of angular-momentum conservation.
- $T^2\propto a^3$ uses the semi-major axis, not the instantaneous distance.

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: Objective I
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Newton's law of universal gravitation: $$F = \frac{Gm_1m_2}{r^2}$$ where $G = 6.674\times10^{-11}\,\rm N\,m^2\,kg^{-2}$ is the universal gravitational constant. The force is: - attractive (along the line joining the masses) - proportional to the product of masses - inversely proportional to the square of the distance Answer: (C).
- Escape velocity is found by setting total mechanical energy = 0 (just barely escapes to infinity): $$\tfrac{1}{2}mv_e^2 - \frac{GMm}{R} = 0 \implies v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2GM}{R}}$$ For Earth: $v_e \approx 11.2\,\rm km/s$. Note: $g = GM/R^2$, so $v_e = \sqrt{2gR}$. Answer: (B).
- **Kepler's third law** (law of periods): The square of the orbital period $T$ is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis $r$: $$T^2 \propto r^3 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{T^2}{r^3} = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM_{\rm Sun}} = \rm const.$$ Derivation: for a circular orbit, gravitational force = centripetal force: $$\frac{GMm}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r} = \frac{m(2\pi r/T)^2}{r} \implies T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GM}$$ Answer: (B).
- For circular orbit, gravitational force = centripetal force: $$\frac{GMm}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r} \implies v^2 = \frac{GM}{r} \implies v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}}$$ Note: $v = v_e/\sqrt{2}$. As $r$ increases, orbital speed **decreases** ã satellites closer to Earth orbit faster. For a near-Earth orbit ($r \approx R_E$), $v \approx 7.9\,\rm km/s$. Answer: (A).
- The exact gravitational potential energy (with $U \to 0$ as $r \to \infty$): $$U = -\frac{GMm}{R+h}$$ The negative sign indicates a bound state. The approximation $U \approx mgh$ holds only when $h \ll R$: $$\Delta U = -\frac{GMm}{R+h} + \frac{GMm}{R} \approx \frac{GMmh}{R^2} = mgh$$ Answer: (B).
- Gravity at height $h$: $g_h = GM/(R+h)^2 = g\,R^2/(R+h)^2$. Set $g_h = g/4$: $$\frac{R^2}{(R+h)^2} = \frac{1}{4} \implies (R+h)^2 = 4R^2 \implies R+h = 2R \implies h = R$$ So at altitude $h = R_E$ (one Earth radius above the surface), $g$ is reduced to $g/4$. Answer: (A).
- A **geostationary satellite** must: 1. Have an orbital period equal to Earth's rotation period: **$T = 24$ hours** (more precisely, 23 h 56 min = sidereal day). 2. Orbit in the **equatorial plane** (inclination = 0ô¯) in the same direction as Earth's rotation. 3. Orbit at the specific radius $r \approx 42,\!000\,\rm km$ ($\approx 6.6\,R_E$) where Kepler's third law gives $T = 24\,\rm h$. From Earth, it appears **stationary** ã used for communication and weather satellites. Answer: (B).

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: Objective II
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- - **A** ã: $V = -GM/r < 0$ for all finite $r > 0$. - **B** ã: As $r$ increases, $|V| = GM/r$ decreases, so $V$ increases toward zero. - **C** ã: $\lim_{r\to\infty} V = 0$ ã the reference point for gravitational potential energy is at infinity. - **D** ã: Gravitational field (force per unit mass) $g = -dV/dr = -GM/r^2 \cdot (-1)\cdot(-1)$... more precisely $\vec{g} = -\nabla V$; in 1D: $g = -dV/dr = -GM/r^2$ (directed inward). Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D).
- - **A** ã: $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$ ã lower $r$ ã higher $v$. - **B** ã: $T \propto r^{3/2}$ ã lower $r$ ã shorter $T$. - **C** ã: Total energy $E = KE + PE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 - GMm/r = \tfrac{GMm}{2r} - \tfrac{GMm}{r} = -\tfrac{GMm}{2r} < 0$. A bound orbit has **negative** total energy. - **D** ã: For a circular orbit: $KE = GMm/(2r)$ and $|PE| = GMm/r = 2\,KE$. This is the **virial theorem** for a $1/r^2$ force: $KE = -E = |PE|/2$. Answer: (A), (B), (D).

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: How one inverse-square law explains gravity from falling apples to orbits
summary: Newton's 1/rôý law underlies surface gravity, potential energy, escape speed, satellite orbits and Kepler's laws ã all flowing from F = GMm/rôý.
body:
**Must know**
- Surface gravity $g=\dfrac{GM}{R^2}$; potential energy $U=-\dfrac{GMm}{r}$.
- Escape speed $v_e=\sqrt{2gR}$; orbital speed $v_o=\sqrt{GM/r}$; orbital energy $E=-\dfrac{GMm}{2r}$.
- Kepler's third law $T^2\propto a^3$.
**Exam traps**
- Keep escape speed ($\sqrt{2}\,v_o$) distinct from orbital speed.
- The energy of a bound orbit is negative.

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: Why every mass attracts every other as an inverse square
summary: Newton's law of gravitation: any two point masses attract each other along the line joining them with a force F = Gmãmã/rôý.
body:
**Must know**
- $F=\dfrac{Gm_1m_2}{r^2}$, with $G=6.67\times10^{-11}\ \text{N m}^2\text{kg}^{-2}$.
- The force is always attractive and acts along the line joining the centres; for spheres use the centre-to-centre distance.
- It is an inverse-square force, independent of the intervening medium.
**Exam traps**
- $G$ is a universal constant; $g$ is a local quantity that depends on the planet.
- A uniform spherical shell attracts an external point as if all its mass were at the centre, and exerts zero force inside.

unitLabel: Unit V ã Systems & Rigid Bodies
title: reNEET2026
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- $v_{esc}=\sqrt{2GM/R}\propto1/\sqrt R$. Equal mass with $R_2=R_1/2\Rightarrow v_2/v_1=\sqrt{R_1/R_2}=\sqrt2.$
- Kepler's third law: $T^2\propto R^3\Rightarrow T\propto R^{3/2}.$
